The chair said, the budget is approved.
Izabel, I missed the board meeting. Can you relay what was said?
Of course. The chair said that the year had been strong, and he explained that the sensor business had grown faster than the board expected. I circulated the minutes this morning.
Good. And what did the CFO report about the numbers?
She briefed the board and gave a short recap. She reported that the margin had improved, but she warned that overhead would rise if we opened a second plant too soon.
And the client on the call, what did they ask?
They asked whether we could shorten the lead time, and whether we would commit to a firm date. The board agreed to clarify both points before signing.
So what did the board finally decide?

They reached a consensus: they would endorse the launch, but defer the new plant for six months. I told the operations lead to review the schedule, and the key takeaway was to confirm the numbers first.
I told the operations lead to review the schedule.
The board reached a consensus to endorse the launch.

Hi Izabel, this is Grace, the board secretary, with a recap of Thursday's meeting before I circulate the full minutes. The chair opened by saying that the year had been strong, and he explained that the sensor business had grown faster than anyone had expected. He added that the North American launch was now the top priority. The CFO then briefed the board on the numbers. She reported that the margin had improved, but she warned that overhead would rise if we opened a second plant too soon. On the client, you told the board that they had asked whether AVRT could shorten its lead time, and whether we would commit to a firm delivery date. The board agreed to clarify those two points before signing. In the end, everyone reached a consensus: they would endorse the launch, but defer the second plant for six months. I have two action items for you: the operations lead was asked to review the delivery schedule, and finance was asked to circulate an updated forecast. The key takeaway, as you put it, was that the board backs the plan but wants the numbers confirmed first. I will send the minutes this afternoon.

Izabel, it is Oscar, your advisor, with a quick debrief after the client call. Let me relay what they actually said, because it matters. Their head of procurement told me that the technical team had loved the sensor, and she admitted that your quality was ahead of every rival she had tested. Then she asked whether we could move the delivery date forward by three weeks, and whether the price would hold if the volume doubled. I explained that you would not cut the price, but that you would protect the lead time. She said that was fair. One warning: their CEO added that a competitor had approached them last month, so I would not defer our answer for long. My advice, and I put this in the debrief note I will circulate: brief your operations lead today, clarify the delivery date by Friday, and endorse the deal before the competitor does. The takeaway is simple. They want us, but they want speed. Call me after you have read the minutes.
